title: Mine Closure Frameworks: RECLAIM, L.I.V.E.S., and ARTHA
focus: Frameworks and guides for responsible mine closures and green financing.
rationale: This section covers specific frameworks and guidelines introduced for sustainable mine closure and financing, forming a distinct examinable cluster.
sectionExcerpt: RECLAIM Framework for Mine Closure: REACH OUT, ENVISION, CO DESIGN, LOCALISE, ACT, INTEGRATE, MAINTAIN. L.I.V.E.S. and ARTHA unveiled at Star Rating Award Ceremony for Coal and Lignite Mines. L.I.V.E.S is a comprehensive practitioner’s guide to serve as benchmark for responsible and sustainable mine closures. ARTHA is a Green Financing Framework for channeling investments towards transforming reclaimed mines into productive and environmentally friendly assets.
evidenceBullets: RECLAIM stands for Reach out, Envision, Co-design, Localise, Act, Integrate, Maintain.
L.I.V.E.S is a practitioner's guide serving as a benchmark for responsible and sustainable mine closures.
ARTHA is a Green Financing Framework designed to transform reclaimed mines into productive assets.

title: Uniform Renewable Energy Tariff (URET)
focus: The scrapping of URET by the Ministry of Power and its implications.
rationale: A specific policy decision regarding renewable energy pricing that was revoked, making it a standalone conceptual point.
sectionExcerpt: Uniform Renewable Energy Tariff (URET) scrapped by Ministry of Power. Aims to provide uniform tariff to consumers by averaging competitive bid tariffs of similar renewable energy projects within central pools. Reasons for revocation: Procurers were reluctant to sign Power Sale Agreements due to uncertainty of tariffs over the three-year fixed period causing stalled projects.
evidenceBullets: URET was scrapped by the Ministry of Power.
It aimed to average competitive bid tariffs of similar RE projects to provide a uniform tariff.
It was revoked because procurers were reluctant to sign Power Sale Agreements due to tariff uncertainty over a three-year fixed period.

title: UNDP Equator Initiative Award
focus: Recognition of Bibi Fatima SHG and the Equator Initiative's mandate.
rationale: An important international biodiversity and community-led conservation award with a specific Indian winner.
sectionExcerpt: Bibi Fatima Women’s self-help group (SHG) from a small village in Dharwad district was one of the winners of the ‘Equator Initiative Award’, also referred to as Nobel Prize for Biodiversity Conservation. Presented biennially under Equator Initiative of United Nations Development Programme (UNDP). The initiative provides opportunities for Indigenous Peoples and local communities to address land degradation, biodiversity conservation and livelihood improvement.
evidenceBullets: Bibi Fatima Women's SHG from Dharwad won the UNDP Equator Initiative Award.
The award is also referred to as the Nobel Prize for Biodiversity Conservation.
It is presented biennially by the UNDP to recognize Indigenous Peoples and local communities.

title: Sustainable Energy for All (SEforALL) & UNOPS
focus: NTPC's tie-up with SEforALL and the organizational details of UNOPS.
rationale: Covers an international energy transition organization and its parent UN agency details, relevant for international organizations questions.
sectionExcerpt: NTPC ties up with SEforALL for energy transition roadmap. Launched by former UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon in 2011. It is an independent organization, hosted by UNOPS. Global mandate: Accelerate progress on energy transition in emerging and developing countries. About UN office for Project Services (UNOPS): Now a separate, self-financing UN Agency which was part of UNDP until 1994. Headquarter: Copenhagen, Denmark.
evidenceBullets: NTPC partnered with SEforALL to create an energy transition roadmap.
SEforALL is an independent organization hosted by UNOPS.
UNOPS is a self-financing UN Agency headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark.

title: Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) and Renewable Consumption Obligation (RCO)
focus: Transition from RPO to RCO under the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
rationale: Core statutory obligations for renewable energy consumption involving multiple acts (ECA 2001, Electricity Act 2003) and regulatory bodies.
sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Power issued a revised draft notification on RCO under Energy Conservation Act (ECA) 2001 establishing mandatory year-wise Renewable Energy (RE) consumption targets from 2024-25 to 2029-30. RPO, established under Electricity Act, 2003, mandates electricity distribution companies, captive power producers and open-access consumers to annually purchase a certain minimum amount of RE. While RPO focuses on actual purchase, RCO focuses on actual consumption from RE sources. Creates binding obligations with penalties for non-compliance overseen by Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).
evidenceBullets: RCO draft notification was issued under the Energy Conservation Act (ECA) 2001.
RPO was established under the Electricity Act, 2003.
RPO focuses on the purchase of electricity from RE sources, whereas RCO focuses on actual consumption.
Penalties for non-compliance with RCO are overseen by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).

title: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)
focus: SAF blending mandates, feedstocks, and the Indian Oil-Air India MoU.
rationale: Emerging alternative fuel technology with specific blending targets and feedstock details.
sectionExcerpt: Indian Oil signed a MoU with Air India for supply of sustainable aviation fuel. An alternative fuel made from non-petroleum feedstocks that reduces emissions from air transportation. Can be blended up to 50% in conventional aviation turbine fuel (ATF or jet fuel). India mandated 1% SAF blending in jet fuel sold to international airlines from 2027. Raw material: Corn grain, Oil seeds, Algae, Agricultural residues, Forestry residues, Wastewater treatment sludge etc.
evidenceBullets: Indian Oil and Air India signed an MoU for the supply of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF).
SAF can be blended up to 50% in conventional aviation turbine fuel.
India has mandated a 1% SAF blending in jet fuel for international airlines starting in 2027.
SAF is produced from non-petroleum feedstocks like corn grain, algae, and agricultural residues.

title: Green Municipal Bonds (GMB)
focus: India's first certified GMB by Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam and municipal bond basics.
rationale: Financial instrument for urban environmental projects with constitutional backing (Article 243W).
sectionExcerpt: Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam pioneered India's first certified GMB issued under Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban, raising ₹150 crore for development of Tertiary Sewage Treatment Plant (TSTP). TSTP was developed under Public-Private Hybrid Annuity Model (PPP-HAM). Municipal bonds refer to non-convertible debt securities issued by municipal body or others established for such purposes and entrusted with functions under Article 243W of Constitution. Green bonds are used to raise funds specifically for climate mitigation, adaptation and other environment friendly projects.
evidenceBullets: Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam issued India's first certified Green Municipal Bond.
The funds raised were used for a Tertiary Sewage Treatment Plant under the PPP-HAM model.
Municipal bonds are non-convertible debt securities issued by bodies entrusted with functions under Article 243W of the Constitution.
